sentences.text: My affidavit, I can't give you exact dates and so forth, when this arrived.
Because I was in the meantime, I was trying to get-- I think I mentioned that to you I was trying to become a cabinet maker.
One of my uncles knew a cabinet maker.
And I went there and started as an apprentice.
And I stayed there with this cabinet maker for about six or seven months.
You see, in the meantime, all kinds of things happened that [INAUDIBLE] happen.
I was trying to go to England.
That didn't work out.
And I was, for the Youth Aliyah the [NON-ENGLISH] Aliyah.
I think I was getting too old, I think.
That didn't work out.
So, eventually, there was nothing else left for me but to wait for the-- for the affidavit.
And I registered with the American consulate.
And that takes-- that took time.
You see, there were thousands and thousands of people who had also affidavits.
And there were only so many doctors at the American consulate.
Because the Americans were quite strict, you know, with tuberculosis and stuff like that.
And I was waiting.
I was waiting.
Now, in the meantime, what else happened?
Yeah.
By the way, I'd also joined one of the Zionist organizations.
Because that was the thing to do.
And I went even to a camp, a camp in the country.
And one fine day, it's just a funny thing.
It was a camp and in the-- in the-- in the country.
It was a Jewish youth organization called Blau Weiss, which was blue white.
And in Hebrew, it was called [NON-ENGLISH].
I think it's associated with a labor party, you see.
And one fine day, two SS men came to inspect the local library.
One of them was a Mr.
Eichmann.
He-- because he was the Jewish expert.
So he looked at all the books that were in the library.
Somebody said, Herr Eichmann-- Herr Eichmann is here to inspect the library.
So we-- I think I met-- I think I saw him once when he was there.
That's all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Anyway, so then what happened next? so 1938 went into 1939.
Then Czechoslovakia was occupied, where my aunt-- where we had many relatives, and then the summer of 1939.
And then, of course, on September the '39, in September of 1939, the war broke out.
But even at that time it was still possible to emigrate to the United States.
Because the United States was neutral.
And you could travel to the United States via Holland.
That was the way to go, the Dutch ships going to the United States from Holland.
So I was still waiting.
I was still waiting for the-- for the-- my-- my letter from the American consulate.
But what happened then next was this.
Then the-- the war broke out, and Poland was overwhelmed, of course, in a couple of-- in a few weeks.
What happened next was the following thing.
I got a letter from the Jewish community council-- not a letter, a postcard-- that I have to appear with 50 kilo luggage for transportation to Poland.
That was in-- in-- that was in-- end of September or early of October of 1939.
Now, we were really stunned, only I, not my parents.
And, apparently, as I found out later on, only it was mostly young man who got this thing.
As we found out later, it was supposed to be-- they wanted to send a few thousand.
They wanted to-- I think the Nazis wanted to establish a Jewish reservation in the Lublin area.
And they were going to send a few thousand people ahead to build all those camps and stuff like that.
But I didn't want to go to Poland, even though there was no question of Holocaust or anything like this.
But somehow I didn't want to go to Poland with a mass transport.
Who knows what's going to happen there?
I don't know.
So, of course, I didn't want to go.
My parents didn't want to go.
Nobody wanted to go.
But what do you do?
You have this letter.
If you don't show up, the-- the-- if you-- there's a list.
It was all organized.
The Jews were told to collect.
You see, I got the letter not from the Gestapo.
I got a letter from the Jewish community council.
But the Jewish community council was ordered to do that.
So if the-- if the Jewish community didn't deliver me on that particular day, I would be picked up by the police.
Because they would have to hand my name over to the police that I didn't show up.
So what happened was my parents were running around.
My father and I were running around.
Is there any way of going quickly out?
Because I can't wait for the American visa anymore.
So we found out at that time, which was in October or November, that there are these illegal transports going to Palestine, illegal transports.
So I-- my father-- and there are only a certain number of places left on this transport.
So we found out that there are few places left from the Mizrachi Organization.
You know the Mizrachi?
It's the religious Jewish, religious Zionist organization.
They had about four or five places left.
And if you pay them, pay the Mizrachi 2,000 marks, you're gone.
So we went to the Mizrachi.
The man looks at me.
There were all these religious Jews there.
We, of course, not religious.
But-- he says to me, you [NON-ENGLISH] every day?
I, of course, I lied like a-- like a-- I lied.
I said, of course, every day.
I don't even know what that was.
I know what it was, but I didn't, you know?
We were not religious at all.
We didn't go to-- never go to the synagogue.
Although we are living next to a synagogue, I never went to that synagogue, except when a-- when a-- if somebody got married or something.
Anyway, the fact is, I got all the paperwork was done within a week or so.
I get my passport with the exit permit with a false visa to Paraguay, which is in my passport even today.
And I left Vienna for Bratislava.
By myself.
By myself with a few other young men.
We had about three or four other people on the train for Bratislava, which was at that time-- Czechoslovakia didn't exist anymore.
But there was an independent state of Slovakia, which was like a German satellite state.
And these transports were being assembled in Bratislava.
And then we're supposed to go down the Danube to Romania, and from there by ship to-- to Palestine.
There was no Israel, of course, at the time.
And so I arrived in Bratislava.
And we were picked up and brought to this camp, where there were already about 200 or 300 people mostly from Vienna, who were also waiting for that transport to get organized.
Now, that was-- I left Vienna.
And I had the date, of course.
I left Vienna on December 23, 1939, a day just before Christmas.
I still remember it.
I have it stamped in my passport.
[LAUGHS] So there's no question about it.
So I left Bratislava.
And arrived-- we arrived there.
And we stayed there-- I stayed there at the camp waiting for the transport to get organized.
Because we are not just 300.
There was supposed to be 1,000, or I don't know how many people are going-- supposed to go down.
Of course, this all was done with the permission of the German authorities.
Because they knew I wasn't going to Paraguay with a false visa there.
Well, they want to get rid of the Jews.
The policy at that time was not to exterminate the Jews.
The-- the idea was to get rid of the Jews, to send them to England, to anywhere to-- to America, just to get rid of them.
You see?
It was-- [LAUGHS] it was an abandoned ammunition factory.
And it was-- we were living in this abandoned ammunition factory called the Patronka.
And there were about 300 or 400 people there.
And it was the winter.
The winter was extremely cold.
And the place was very badly heated.
Let me tell you.
It was so cold, we were sleeping on the floor on straw, by the way.
But there was a huge room there.
Because it was an abandoned factory.
So it was this huge room, only men in this room.
And the women were in another room.
It was so cold, I was hanging my-- my winter coat on-- on the window like on a little nail.
In the morning it was frozen to the window.
So for what we did the next time, we got some wood, some boards, and we nailed wood across and filled the space between the boards and the window, filled with sawdust.
Because it-- the window, some of the panes were broken and stuff like that.
So we were able to-- to survive.
But the winter was extremely cold.
Now, what happened further with the winter was that the Danube froze.
As a result, because you see we were supposed to go on the Danube.
Because on the Danube, if you go on the Danube, in those years the Danube was an international river.
And you didn't need any transit visas to go through Hungary, Yugoslavia, Romania, or Bulgaria.
As long as you were traveling on the Danube, you didn't need anything.
It was an international river that was a legacy of the First World War of the Versailles Treaty, or whatever.
But the Danube froze, so we got stuck in-- in-- in Bratislava, or Pressburg as its known in German.
What happened in the meantime, my parents got a letter from the American consulate in-- in January or February of 1940.
The letter arrived from the American consulate, I should appear at the American consulate for my medical exam to go to America.
But I wasn't in Vienna.
I couldn't go back.
So my parents-- there was no American consulate in Bratislava.
There was only one in Budapest.
But it was difficult to get to Budapest.
You'd need a visa.
It's impossible.
So my parents went.
And my parents knew I was stuck in Bratislava.
Maybe we'll never be able to get from there to Palestine.
My parents went to the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish community, and-- and explained the situation.
And the [NON-ENGLISH], they went to see the Gestapo.
And the Gestapo helped them.
The Gestapo sent, allowed, a Jewish functionary from the Jewish community council to go to Bratislava and pick me up.
Not only me [INAUDIBLE], but there were also two other people who were in the same position.
There were-- we were three young men all about 18-19, to go back to Vienna with the permission of the Gestapo to get the American visa, so we could go to America.
You see, it's very interesting.
These-- you don't-- people don't know about those things, the Gestapo helping Jews, you know?
Anyway, the Jewish functionary arrived in Bratislava.
He-- we picked up our-- we picked our bags-- we packed our bags and went back to Vienna.
But we tried to get back to Vienna.
At the German border, we were stopped by the German passport control.
He looks at my passport.
And he says, look, with this passport it says here, you can only leave Germany once.
And you can never come back.
That's what it said there.
You see what I mean?
But the functionary said, it says, here, I have the letter from the Gestapo.
He say, Gestapo, schmepapo.
I am not-- I am not interested in the Gestapo.
I am a border passport control.
Here, it says in the-- in the passport, you can't come back.
And I don't care what the Gestapo says.
You can't come back.
I have nothing against you personally.
He was very nice, actually.
He says, look.
I have nothing against you, Jewish, or non-Jewish.
It says here you can't come back.
So you can't-- you can't come back.
Never mind what the Gestapo says.
The fellow from Vienna was allowed to go back to Vienna.
Because he hadn't-- he didn't have that stamp in his passport.
But we were not allowed to go back to Germany.
You know I mean?
So what happened, we were stopped on the border.
We were put into a room there.
And they made some jokes with us.
We had to crawl under the bed.
Some other people came, some nasty Nazis came.
They told me to crawl under the bed and start singing.
Then he said to me, OK, jump.
Start jumping.
I had to jump, and then I had to do this and that.
But I wasn't beaten up.
I must say, I wasn't beaten up.
And then I was sent back to-- so on the same day, I arrived back in Bratislava in the camp again, after my adventure with the-- with the German.
After that, I said, I'll never go back to this bloody country again, you know after that experience.
[LAUGHS] So that was-- that was-- that was that.
Now that was-- it must have been February or March of 1939.
And we were still were stuck in this-- the Danube was by then, unfrozen.
But they still were not able to arrange all the ships and everything and so on and so forth.
Then it was summer of 1940.
And then France was defeated.
And-- and the Germans occupied practically the whole of Europe.
But that didn't affect us, actually.

And then I got a letter one fine day from my parents that my parents told me that another transport is going to Palestine, another illegal transport from Vienna, which could-- which is probably in combination with mine.
And they are going to join that one, too.
By that time, it was late in the summer.
And I got the-- like I said, I got a postcard from my parents.
They are definitely going on that transport.
And, eventually, that transport was put together by some underground Jewish underground organization, with of course, all with the permission of the Gestapo, all with the permission of Mr.
Eichmann and all these people.
And there was a transport of about 3,600 people.
Imagine a huge illegal transport leaving Germany in the middle of the war for enemy [INAUDIBLE], for Palestine, with the permission of the Germans.
OK?
There were about 900 Jews from Vienna.
By the time in Bratislava, we were about 300 in our camp.
And there was a second camp in Bratislava with about 400, also all Jews from Czechoslovakia.
Then 600 were supposed to come from Danzig.
And-- and some-- and, also, it's I don't know how many hundreds from Germany, for a total of about 3,500 or 3,600 people.
And there were all supposed to travel down the Danube on about four-- on four ships.
Because you can't get a ship that size.
So there were four ships.

One-- the one from Vienna was called the I was on the with 1,000, the Helios, and the There were four ships, the the the and the So my parents-- and all of these ships sailed roughly at the same time.
My parents sailed from Vienna on the with 900 other people.
I sailed from Bratislava with 1,000 people.
And the others also came from Danzig.
They came by train with a special train with a sealed train from Danzig to Bratislava.
They entered the ships at Bratislava.

And that whole convoy of about four ships sailed down the Danube from-- to-- to-- Budapest and all down the Danube through Belgrade, Yugoslavia, Romania, Bulgaria, until all the ships arrived in Romania near the Black Sea.
Yeah.
Yes.
OK.
I can
Sure.
[LAUGHS] The-- the camp was, like I said, it was in a disused factory.
We were not allowed to go out.
We were under the-- under-- under guard of the Slovak-- the so-called Hlinka Guard, which is really like storm-- the Slovak storm troopers.
But they were not too bad.
You could pay them off or something like this.
But we never-- we were never allowed to go into town, except with special permission.
And I had an uncle in-- in Slovakia.
He came one day.
And I was allowed to leave the camp for two weeks.
And I stayed with my uncle in Slovakia for two weeks.
He did not survive the war.
He and his wife were sent to Auschwitz.
But, anyway-- and we were in the camp.
In the camp, there were two-- [LAUGHS] in the camp, there were two kitchens, a kosher kitchen and a treifa kitchen.
When I arrived, they asked me, you're kosher or treifa?
So I said, why?
Where is the better food?
I said, well-- they said, the-- the treifa have more meat.
And the kosher have better pastries.
So I said, I go for the treifa.
[LAUGHS] So I-- So what did they do?
They put me into the kosher kitchen, of course.
I said, treifa, they put into the kosher kitchen.
This is-- this is typical.
You know what I mean?
[LAUGHS] So I was in the-- and there, we stayed.
I stayed there in this blasted camp from December of 1939, because I arrived in December 1939, till September.
I was there for nine months in this camp.
And we, eventually, we did some work.
And we-- we worked in the garden.
And we did grow some vegetables.
But, basically, it was very boring.
The morale was low.
Because we-- there was continuously the danger that we would be sent back, and that we would not be able to-- to make it.
We were supposed to go to Palestine.
And the Slovaks didn't want us to go there.
The Slovaks were paid off by the Join again.
The Joint provides the money for our food and everything.
The Joint provided that.
We didn't know about it.
But I found out about it later.
The Joint provided the dollars to-- to-- to have us fed.
You see what I mean?
That was paid.
You know, there is some literature on this, on who-- who-- who supported us.
You were allowed to send letters home.
Yes.
Yes.
I was-- I still have some postcards, I think.
I was-- yes.
There was-- it was possible to send the letters.
I even had some relatives in Bratislava on my mother's side.
And one-- one or two days, I was allowed to visit my relatives in Bratislava.
Also, they did not survive.
Not one of them survived.
I have to refresh my memory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the convoy left Bratislava on September 3, 1940.
Well, the-- the ship was a Danube ship.
It was a very nice ship, like a paddle wheeler.
You know, on the Danube, they had paddle wheelers.
And we were-- we were-- it was very crowded, but was very nice.
[INAUDIBLE] the weather was perfect and we were sailing down the Danube, which is very picturesque.
And we had a great time.
[INAUDIBLE] The food was pretty good.
It was very crowded.
I was sleeping on deck.
You know.
But who the hell cares?
I mean, I was a young, young man.
It this very crowded and-- and maybe the toilet facilities were not, because it was so overcrowded.
You see what I mean?
A ship like this only-- only carries maybe 200 people.
We were 1,000.
But it was OK.
In-- in-- in Bulgaria, the ships were stopped for a certain period of time.
And my parents' ship-- and our-- my parents were on another ship, right?
I was on the My parents were on the The ships were lying next to each other.
And I jumped over, and I visited my parents, the first time seeing my parents in nine months.
My mother had, by the way, tried to persuade her sister to join the transport, but she refused.
They said, look, we are--- we are retired.
We're living in our own house.
And what can happen?
What can happen to us?
Nothing.
Yeah, we are retired.
My-- you know, but they didn't survive.
Yeah.
How long were you on the ship?
[LAUGHS] Well, the ship sailed from Bratislava on September 3.
And it arrived in Tulcea, which is the Danube-- the Danube estuary on September 11.
So that's-- it took quite some time to get-- get down the Danube.
And what happened next was there were three high-sea ships standing in the Danube estuary, three Greek ships.
But they didn't have the Greek flag.
They had the Panama flag, which is like a flag of convenience.
Because it was all illegal.
The-- the Greek government did not allow-- did not allow this kind of thing.
So they got the ships registered under Panama.
Under the Panama flag, you can do anything, practically, you know, even probably today.
I don't know.
But anyway, so my parents were on one ship, the 1,700 people.
I was on another ship, the with 1,000 people.
And there was a third ship, the with about 900 people.
So they switched you to these ships at that point.
Yes, from the Danube ship.
From the Danube ships, we were switched to those ships.
And the interesting thing is, the Germans are very clever.
These-- these Danube ships, on the way back, they were filled with the so-called Volksdeutsche, who were evacuated from Russia at that time.
Remember, Germany and Russia was at that time allies, right?
And the Germans were, the Volksdeutsche, the German-speaking people from Russia, were going back to the-- back in the [NON-ENGLISH], go back-- going back to Germany.
So the Jews paid for the-- the-- the repatriation of the Germans from Russia.
You see what I mean?
So anyway, we were-- we were-- I was on one ship.
My parents were on another  ship.
Now, what happened next was that my parents went to see our-- the leaders of the transport and said, look, we are on this ship here.
Our son is on another ship.
Is it possible to get him transferred from one ship to the other with the permission of the Romanian Port Authority?
Because you're not allowed to go back and forth.
You know, there were Romanian police boats going around there that nobody should go on land, God forbid.
Do you know what I mean?
So they arranged with the Romanian authorities.
There was an exchange made.
And one fine day, somebody came.
Listen, your parents made arrangements to go to the other ship.
Pack your bags, and go to the other ship.
So I packed my bags.
I had only one small suitcase and a-- and a backpack.
And I joined my parents on the on the-- which was the biggest ship with about 1,000 tons with 1,800 people.
I mean, the-- the overcrowding is difficult to describe, let me tell you.
Try.
Try?
At one time while traveling, it was so crowded that the ship started to lean over to one side.
It leaned over.
Because it was-- it was-- how shall I put it?
There was too much weight near the top.
It leaned over to one side.
Now, we had a-- we had a police on board of the ship of our own, like an order group.
They were stationed at strategic points to-- to see that the traffic goes and so forth.
I was also a member of that group.
And what-- what-- when that-- that happened quite frequently that the ship started to lean over to this side or to this side.
So at that time, the order was everybody to the other side.
[GERMAN].
The-- the language on board the ship was German.
All these Jews were from Central Europe.
[NON-ENGLISH].
It means everybody to the left or everybody to the right, whatever necessary.
And then the ship slowly righted itself.
[LAUGHS] Also, it was so crowded that two people couldn't pass each other.
So as a result, it was organized that if you want to go in this direction on deck, on a certain deck, you could go only in this direction.
And the-- and to go back the other direction, you had to go on the other side of the ship.
It was, you might say what was one-way traffic.
The toilet facilities?
[LAUGHS] The toilet facilities, there was some toilets there, but only for women with small children.
The rest of the toilets there was at the stern of the ship, that is to say the back of the ship.
There were about eight toilets that were overhanging the water.
They were kind of-- you went to the toilet.
It went straight into the water.
And there was-- there was on one side, there was a lineup for men.
And then on other side, there was a lineup.
There was always a lineup.
Because everybody had diarrhea.
Because of the-- the food, and the-- the-- the lack of water.
I don't know.
Anyway, I had-- everybody had diarrhea.
So as a result, there were always lineups.
And [LAUGHS] my mother-- my mother was a-- my mother also was an order, order guard.
And for the ladies, because you had to go quickly, you had to say whether you go big or small.
And if you went to-- if you want to be small, you had to go small.
But some people cheated.
They said they're going small, but they actually went big.
And my mother caught one of those.
And my mother told me, she said, [INAUDIBLE] I caught this one.
She said she was going small, but she went actually big.
So she's says-- my mother said, I gave her hell.
[LAUGHTER]
So I mean, the-- the-- it was difficult to describe the-- the overcrowding.
I mean, some of those pictures that you've seen-- but it's-- it's difficult to describe.
Below deck there were-- where I was living, way down below, there were three tiers of bunks.
And some of these people-- I'm telling you the truth.
Some of these people like had-- they had diarrhea.
And it was dripping.
I swear.
It was dripping down from the top in some cases.
You know what I mean?
People couldn't-- couldn't restrain themselves.
That-- that happened.
Not very often, but it did happen sometimes.
So it was terrible.
And the food was, of course, you had to line up for the food all the time.
There were continuous lineups.
Because there were-- there was some food.
It was very bad.
And it was not enough.
And there was not enough water.
And there was not enough food.
And the food was very bad.
For instance, after a while the bread gave out.
And we had biscuits.
And I remember, let me tell you, I remember distinctly it was very damp.
These biscuits that we had, they turned green.
And I had-- I remember I had a brush, a steel brush to brush the shoes.
And I brushed off the green with that stuff, you know, with the brush.
And that's where I got the diarrhea, I'm sure, from-- from that stuff.
Yes.
There was.
There were about five or six doctors there.
We had a hospital on-- on board.
But during the trip, about 12 people died of typhus.
But that was kept secret.
We didn't even know about that.
Because, otherwise, there would have been a panic.
So nobody said anything.
But I found that out.
We found that out later on that a few people died of typhus, because of those terrible sanitary conditions.
We couldn't wash.
There were no washing facilities.
The only way to wash yourself was everybody had a little can made from a-- from a can with a string.
I used to throw that overboard and pull the water up from-- but that was salt water, of course, you know.
No.
There was only the Greek crew.
Oh, they were quite nice.
The captain was a crook.
But the crew, there was just a few-- a few crew members, not many, maybe 10 or 15.
And they kept to themselves.
And we had a leadership.
They-- they negotiated with the captain everything that had to be negotiated with.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Well, let me now start about the journey.
Well, first of all, there were three ships anchored in the estuary of the Danube at a place called Tulcea.
You can find--
Right.
Well, that's good place to stop.
